skill.name: terraform-module-library
skill.description: Build reusable Terraform modules for AWS, Azure, and GCP infrastructure using documented module layouts, examples, validation patterns, and Terratest guidance.
skill.summary: Create reusable Terraform module patterns for multi-cloud infrastructure.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are Markdown examples, Terraform snippets, private CIDR examples, or ordinary documentation text rather than executable malware. The remaining risk is operational: copied Terraform and Terratest examples can create or destroy real cloud resources if run without review.

medium_findings.title: Terraform Examples Can Change Real Infrastructure
medium_findings.description: The Terratest example calls terraform.Destroy and terraform.InitAndApply. This is legitimate testing guidance, but copied code can create or destroy cloud resources and incur costs if run against real credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines directly show apply and destroy operations in a Terraform test example. The intent is legitimate, but the operational impact is real when users execute the generated code.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography hits occur in prose, Terraform descriptions, output names, and related-skill text. No MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic implementation was found at the cited locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are documentation text or Terraform identifiers, not cryptographic code. The analyzer appears to have matched substrings such as description or Destroy.

low_findings.title: Command Execution Detections Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The reported backtick and import patterns are Markdown code fences, directory trees, HCL snippets, and a Go import block. They are not skill runtime commands and do not execute by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are fenced documentation blocks and one Go import declaration. No shell execution wrapper or user-input command construction is present in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Network And Path Traversal Detections Are Terraform Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded network values are private RFC1918 CIDR examples. The relative paths are Terraform module source paths and a Terratest example directory, not filesystem traversal in executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context shows private infrastructure examples and relative module references. There is no evidence of file reads, writes, exfiltration, or traversal against user-controlled paths.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detections Are Documentation Text
low_findings.description: The reported reconnaissance patterns are Terraform resource names, output descriptions, and infrastructure terms. No evidence found of host enumeration, environment inspection, or data collection logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are ordinary Terraform VPC fields and documentation. No commands, APIs, or logic collect host or user system details.

dangerous_patterns.title: Terraform Apply And Destroy In Test Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes Terratest calls that apply and later destroy Terraform infrastructure. This pattern is expected for module testing but should be reviewed before execution in live cloud accounts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Terraform apply and destroy calls are explicit. The risk depends on user execution context and cloud credentials, not on hidden skill behavior.

content.value_statement: Teams need consistent Terraform modules that work across cloud projects. This skill provides reusable AWS, Azure, and GCP module patterns with examples, validation guidance, and testing practices.
actual_capabilities: Defines a standard Terraform module directory structure with variables, outputs, examples, and tests.
Provides AWS VPC module examples with resources, variables, outputs, tags, and validation blocks.
Explains module composition using reusable VPC and RDS module references.
Lists AWS module patterns for VPC, EKS, RDS, S3, ALB, Lambda, and security groups.
Suggests Terraform module best practices such as provider pinning, semantic versioning, and Terratest coverage.
limitations: It is guidance and examples, not a complete generated module repository.
It references Azure and GCP patterns but only includes detailed AWS reference content in this package.
Generated Terraform still requires review against your cloud account, provider versions, and security policies.
Terratest examples can create or destroy resources when run with real cloud credentials.

use_cases.title: Standardize Platform Modules
use_cases.description: Create shared VPC, database, cluster, and storage modules that follow one repository layout.

use_cases.title: Bootstrap Cloud Projects
use_cases.description: Draft reusable module scaffolds for new AWS, Azure, or GCP infrastructure projects.

use_cases.title: Improve IaC Reviews
use_cases.description: Compare Terraform module changes against documented practices for inputs, outputs, tags, examples, and tests.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Module
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Terraform module structure for an AWS VPC. Include main files, variables, outputs, an example, and a short README outline.

prompt_templates.title: Add Validation and Outputs
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Terraform module and add useful variable validation, clear output values, provider constraints, and consistent tags.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Multi-Module Stack
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Terraform stack that composes VPC and RDS modules. Explain inputs, outputs, dependencies, and environment-specific variables.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Module Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this Terraform module for reusability, security defaults, documentation quality, testing coverage, and release readiness.

output_examples.input: Ask for a reusable AWS VPC module pattern.
output_examples.output: A module plan with main resources, required variables, useful outputs, validation checks, tagging guidance, and a complete example folder.

output_examples.input: Ask how to compose VPC and RDS modules.
output_examples.output: A composition outline showing module sources, shared network outputs, database inputs, tag propagation, and environment naming conventions.

output_examples.input: Ask for a module review checklist.
output_examples.output: A focused checklist covering provider pinning, semantic versioning, examples, Terratest coverage, input validation, encryption, logging, and least privilege.

best_practices: Review generated Terraform with plan output before applying it to any cloud account.
Pin provider versions and document every variable, output, example, and supported module version.
Test modules in isolated accounts or projects before promoting them to production use.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated apply or destroy commands against production without peer review.
Do not publish modules that hide required inputs or omit security-sensitive defaults.
Do not copy example CIDR ranges, regions, or instance sizes without adapting them to your environment.

faq.question: Does this skill create Terraform files automatically?
faq.answer: It provides patterns and guidance that an AI assistant can use to draft Terraform modules. You still need to review the generated files.

faq.question: Which cloud providers are covered?
faq.answer: The skill describes AWS, Azure, and GCP module organization. The included detailed reference file focuses on AWS module patterns.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatible workflows.

faq.question: Is the Terraform production ready as written?
faq.answer: No. Treat the examples as starting points and validate them against your cloud standards, state setup, and security requirements.

faq.question: Why is the security risk marked medium?
faq.answer: The static malware detections were false positives, but Terraform examples can create or destroy real infrastructure if users run them.

faq.question: Does the skill contain prompt injection text?
faq.answer: No evidence found of prompt injection language in the reviewed files.
